figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of the neural operator surrogate framework for transient flow prediction in the HCSG of an SMR.
Table 1: Summary of related studies on AI-based DT in nuclear systems.
Figure 2: AE architecture: (a) MLP-based AE, (b) convolutional AE.
Figure 3: L-DeepONet architecture. The latent data compressed by the encoder is used to train the DeepONet, and the DeepONet outputs are reconstructed in the original space by the decoder.
Figure 4: Overall architecture of the Fourier Neural Operator.
Table 2: Geometry Parameters
Table 3: Solver Settings

Figure 6: Reference CFD solution at the inlet velocity of 0.4 m/s. (a) Full velocity field u(x, t), illustrating the temporal development of the Kármán vortex street downstream of the cylinder bundle. (b) Temporal difference field ∆u(x, t) = u(x, t)− u0(x), used as the surrogate-model training input. The flow remains relatively uniform at the early time step (t = 2) and progressively develops into a fully periodic vortex streets regime at t = 50 and t = 100.
Figure 7: Mesh resolution comparison near a cylindrical obstacle. (a) 1×, (b) 2×, (c) 3×, (d) 4x.
Figure 8: Probe locations on the unstructured mesh for evaluating the velocity at each node.

Figure 9: Velocity time histories predicted by the vanilla L-DeepONet at probe locations for inlet velocities of (a) 0.4 m/s and (b) 0.7 m/s. Dashed lines indicate reference CFD and solid lines indicate predictions. 23

Figure 10: Comparison of FNO predictions with different Fourier mode configurations at six probe locations for two inlet velocity conditions: (a) 0.4 m/s and (b) 0.7 m/s. The black dashed lines represent the reference CFD solutions, the red solid lines represent the FNO with 12 Fourier modes, and the yellow solid lines represent the FNO with 24 Fourier modes. Doubling the Fourier modes does not significantly improve the prediction of highfrequency oscillatory patterns. 25
Figure 11: Architecture of the proposed Multi-scale L-DeepONet.

Figure 12: Architecture of the Multi-scale FNO (MscaleFNO). Each parallel sub-network receives the scaled initial condition cn · u0(x, y) as input, and their outputs are combined via a weighted summation.
